content.text: Privacy in an LLM system is not a feature you add at the end — it is a system property that must be designed in from the start. It has two dimensions: legal compliance with privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, and others) and technical privacy engineering (minimisation, differential privacy, anonymisation, consent gating). Both are required; neither alone is sufficient.

The central challenge is that LLMs are trained on and process human-generated text, which is almost always personal data under modern privacy laws. Prompts contain names, emails, health details, and financial information. Model outputs can reproduce training-data memorisation. Conversation logs aggregate intimate details over time.

attrs.alt: Privacy rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection, no automated decisions) feed into LLM system; technical controls (PII detection, consent gating, minimisation, differential privacy, audit, encryption) protect the outputs; applicable regulations: GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, PIPEDA, LGPD

content.text: Lawful basis for processing, purpose limitation, data subject rights (Art. 15-22), DPIA for high-risk AI, DPA agreements for sub-processors
Right to know, delete, opt-out of "sale," non-discrimination; applies when processing CA residents' data at scale
PHI cannot be sent to non-HIPAA-covered LLM providers; BAA required; de-identification standard (Safe Harbor or Expert Determination)
Consent required for collection/use/disclosure; accuracy principle; right to access and correct
Closely mirrors GDPR; lawful bases, data subject rights, DPA with supervisory authority
Under GDPR, every processing operation needs a lawful basis. For LLM systems the most common bases are:

from enum import Enum

class LawfulBasis(str, Enum):
    CONSENT            = "consent"              # Art. 6(1)(a) — explicit, withdrawable
    CONTRACT           = "contract"             # Art. 6(1)(b) — necessary to perform a contract
    LEGAL_OBLIGATION   = "legal_obligation"     # Art. 6(1)(c)
    VITAL_INTERESTS    = "vital_interests"      # Art. 6(1)(d)
    PUBLIC_TASK        = "public_task"          # Art. 6(1)(e)
    LEGITIMATE_INTEREST = "legitimate_interest" # Art. 6(1)(f) — requires balancing test

# Map each processing activity to its lawful basis and document the rationale
PROCESSING_REGISTER: list[dict] = [
    {
        "activity": "chat_completion",
        "basis": LawfulBasis.CONTRACT,
        "rationale": "Necessary to provide the service the user signed up for",
        "data_categories": ["chat_content", "user_id"],
        "retention_days": 30,
    },
    {
        "activity": "abuse_monitoring",
        "basis": LawfulBasis.LEGITIMATE_INTEREST,
        "rationale": "Legitimate interest in preventing harm; users notified in privacy policy",
        "data_categories": ["chat_content", "ip_address"],
        "retention_days": 90,
        "lia_completed": True,   # Legitimate Interest Assessment completed
    },
    {
        "activity": "model_fine_tuning",
        "basis": LawfulBasis.CONSENT,
        "rationale": "Users explicitly opted in to contribute data for model improvement",
        "data_categories": ["anonymised_chat_content"],
        "retention_days": 730,
    },
]

Users have the right to request a copy of all personal data you hold about them. For an LLM system this means: conversation history, usage logs, stored preferences, and any inferences derived from their data.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class DataSubjectAccessResponse:
    user_id: str
    requested_at: str
    categories: dict[str, list[Any]]   # category -> list of records
    processing_purposes: list[str]
    retention_periods: dict[str, int]  # category -> days
    third_party_recipients: list[str]

def handle_access_request(user_id: str) -> DataSubjectAccessResponse:
    """
    Compile all personal data held for a user.
    In production: query every data store (DB, cache, logs).
    """
    # Placeholder — replace with real data-store queries
    conversations = _fetch_conversations(user_id)   # list of dicts
    usage_logs = _fetch_usage_logs(user_id)          # scrubbed logs
    preferences = _fetch_preferences(user_id)        # settings, flags

    return DataSubjectAccessResponse(
        user_id=user_id,
        requested_at=_now_iso(),
        categories={
            "conversations": conversations,
            "usage_logs": usage_logs,
            "preferences": preferences,
        },
        processing_purposes=[r["activity"] for r in PROCESSING_REGISTER],
        retention_periods={r["activity"]: r["retention_days"] for r in PROCESSING_REGISTER},
        third_party_recipients=["anthropic (LLM provider, DPA in place)"],
    )

def _fetch_conversations(user_id: str) -> list: return []
def _fetch_usage_logs(user_id: str) -> list: return []
def _fetch_preferences(user_id: str) -> list: return []
def _now_iso() -> str:
    from datetime import datetime
    return datetime.utcnow().isoformat()

Erasure requests must delete data from every store in the system within 30 days (GDPR) or 45 days (CCPA). For LLM systems the hardest part is ensuring that model fine-tuning data is also purged and that log backups are handled.

import hashlib, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass

logger = logging.getLogger("privacy")

@dataclass
class ErasureResult:
    user_id_hash: str
    request_id: str
    stores_cleared: list[str]
    backup_purge_scheduled: bool
    fine_tuning_data_flagged: bool
    completed_at: str

def handle_erasure_request(user_id: str, request_id: str) -> ErasureResult:
    uid_hash = hashlib.sha256(user_id.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]
    stores_cleared = []

    for store in ["conversations", "usage_logs", "prompt_cache", "embeddings"]:
        count = _delete_from_store(store, user_id)
        if count >= 0:
            stores_cleared.append(store)
            logger.info("erasure_store_cleared",
                        extra={"store": store, "user": uid_hash, "count": count})

    # Schedule backup purge (may take up to 30 days for tape/cold storage)
    _schedule_backup_purge(user_id, request_id)

    # Flag any fine-tuning dataset contributions for removal
    ft_flagged = _flag_fine_tuning_data(user_id)

    # Withdraw consent (prevent future processing)
    _withdraw_all_consent(user_id)

    return ErasureResult(
        user_id_hash=uid_hash,
        request_id=request_id,
        stores_cleared=stores_cleared,
        backup_purge_scheduled=True,
        fine_tuning_data_flagged=ft_flagged,
        completed_at=_now_iso(),
    )

def _delete_from_store(store: str, user_id: str) -> int: return 0
def _schedule_backup_purge(user_id: str, request_id: str) -> None: pass
def _flag_fine_tuning_data(user_id: str) -> bool: return True
def _withdraw_all_consent(user_id: str) -> None: pass

When analysing usage patterns (e.g. most common query categories), differential privacy adds calibrated noise to aggregate statistics so no individual's behaviour can be inferred from the results. The Google DP library (python-dp) provides production-ready primitives:

# pip install google-dp
# Demonstrates the concept — adapt to your analytics pipeline
import math, random

def laplace_mechanism(true_value: float, sensitivity: float, epsilon: float) -> float:
    """
    Add Laplace noise to a numeric statistic.
    sensitivity: max change one user can cause in the statistic.
    epsilon: privacy budget (lower = stronger privacy guarantee).
    """
    scale = sensitivity / epsilon
    # Laplace(0, scale) sample via inverse CDF
    u = random.uniform(-0.5, 0.5)
    return true_value - scale * math.copysign(math.log(1 - 2 * abs(u)), u)

def dp_query_count(true_count: int, epsilon: float = 1.0) -> int:
    """Return a differentially private count (sensitivity = 1 for counting queries)."""
    noisy = laplace_mechanism(float(true_count), sensitivity=1.0, epsilon=epsilon)
    return max(0, round(noisy))

# Example: publish daily active user count with ε=1
# true_count = 10_423 -> dp_count ≈ 10_424 ± ~1/ε
These terms are often confused but carry different legal weight under GDPR:
Outside GDPR scope if truly irreversible
Reversible with key; key held separately
Hash or tokenize identifiers; store key separately

import hashlib, secrets, json
from pathlib import Path

class PseudonymVault:
    """Maps real identifiers to pseudonyms; key stored separately from data."""

    def __init__(self, key: bytes):
        self._key = key   # must be stored in a separate secret store

    def pseudonymise(self, real_id: str) -> str:
        import hmac
        mac = hmac.new(self._key, real_id.encode(), hashlib.sha256)
        return "PSE-" + mac.hexdigest()[:16]

    def save_mapping(self, real_id: str, path: Path) -> str:
        pseudo = self.pseudonymise(real_id)
        # In production: store mapping in an access-controlled vault (e.g. HashiCorp Vault)
        mapping = {real_id: pseudo}
        with open(path, "a") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps(mapping) + "\n")
        return pseudo

# Anonymisation check: does the dataset withstand re-identification?
def k_anonymity_check(records: list[dict], quasi_identifiers: list[str], k: int) -> bool:
    """Return True if every equivalence class has at least k members."""
    from collections import Counter
    keys = [tuple(r.get(qi, "") for qi in quasi_identifiers) for r in records]
    counts = Counter(keys)
    return all(c >= k for c in counts.values())
GDPR Article 35 requires a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) before processing that is likely to result in high risk to individuals. LLM deployments almost always qualify. A DPIA documents:
what data is processed, for what purposes, by whom
why the processing is justified and why less invasive alternatives were rejected
enumerated risks to data subjects (profiling, discrimination, disclosure, re-identification)
technical and organisational measures addressing each risk
what risk remains after mitigations, and DPA consultation if high

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class RiskLevel(str, Enum):
    LOW = "low"; MEDIUM = "medium"; HIGH = "high"; CRITICAL = "critical"

@dataclass
class DPIARisk:
    id: str
    description: str
    likelihood: RiskLevel
    impact: RiskLevel
    mitigations: list[str]
    residual_risk: RiskLevel

@dataclass
class DPIA:
    system_name: str
    version: str
    data_categories: list[str]
    processing_purposes: list[str]
    legal_bases: list[str]
    risks: list[DPIARisk]
    dpa_consultation_required: bool

EXAMPLE_DPIA = DPIA(
    system_name="CustomerCare LLM Assistant",
    version="1.0",
    data_categories=["chat_content", "user_id", "support_history"],
    processing_purposes=["customer_support", "abuse_monitoring"],
    legal_bases=["contract", "legitimate_interest"],
    risks=[
        DPIARisk(
            id="R-01",
            description="LLM model memorises and reproduces customer PII from training data",
            likelihood=RiskLevel.MEDIUM,
            impact=RiskLevel.HIGH,
            mitigations=[
                "Training data deduplicated and PII redacted before fine-tuning",
                "Output PII scanner applied to all responses",
                "Canary token memorisation test run monthly",
            ],
            residual_risk=RiskLevel.LOW,
        ),
        DPIARisk(
            id="R-02",
            description="Conversation logs retained indefinitely, enabling profiling",
            likelihood=RiskLevel.HIGH,
            impact=RiskLevel.MEDIUM,
            mitigations=[
                "30-day TTL enforced on conversation logs",
                "Automated purge job runs daily",
                "Users can request immediate deletion",
            ],
            residual_risk=RiskLevel.LOW,
        ),
    ],
    dpa_consultation_required=False,
)
Operational logs are one of the most common sources of accidental PII exposure. Apply these patterns to every log pipeline:

Privacy in LLM systems requires working at two levels simultaneously: legal compliance and technical implementation. On the legal side, identify the lawful basis for every processing activity, implement all data subject rights (access, erasure, portability, rectification, objection), complete a DPIA before deployment, and maintain a processing register. On the technical side, apply PII detection and redaction at every data boundary, enforce consent gating before processing, minimise data collected to what is strictly necessary, use differential privacy for analytics, and make every log pipeline privacy-safe by design. Pseudonymisation is a useful engineering tool but does not remove GDPR obligations; only true anonymisation does. Build privacy in from the start — retrofitting it after deployment is expensive and often incomplete.

Every piece of personal data in your LLM pipeline needs a lawful basis, a stated purpose, a retention limit, and a deletion path. If you cannot document all four for a given data flow, you should not collect it.
